System Design Copilot 0 / 39 lessons complete

System design prep

System Design Copilot

A complete system design interview prep curriculum with topic guides, diagrams, trade-offs, and case studies.

Open curriculum home
Modules 8
Progress 0/39
Foundations and estimation Build the habit of turning vague prompts into concrete requirements, scale assumptions, and explicit trade-offs.
0 / 5 complete
Problem framing and requirements Start every design with the user journey, hard constraints, and the two or three metrics that will define success.Back-of-the-envelope estimation Use quick arithmetic to size read/write traffic, storage growth, cache footprint, and inter-service bandwidth before deep diving.Performance vs. scalability Performance describes how fast one request path is now; scalability describes how gracefully the system behaves as load, data, or team size grows.Latency, throughput, and SLOs Reason about how much work the system can do, how long each request takes, and which percentile targets users actually feel.Availability, consistency, and CAP trade-offs Designs under partition must choose whether to prioritize fresh coordinated answers or continued service with potentially stale data.
Networking, edge, and request routing Learn how requests find your system, how traffic is distributed, and where you terminate policy, caching, and security controls.
0 / 5 complete
DNS fundamentals DNS maps names to endpoints and lets you steer traffic geographically, by health, or by weighted rollout without changing clients.Content delivery networks A CDN moves cacheable content closer to users and can absorb large amounts of read traffic before requests ever reach origin infrastructure.Load balancing Load balancers distribute requests across healthy backends and are the main control point for availability, failover, and safe scaling at the service edge.Reverse proxies, gateways, and edge policy Reverse proxies shape inbound traffic by terminating TLS, applying caching and compression, enforcing auth, and routing to internal services.Rate limiting and edge protection Protect critical services from abusive clients, noisy neighbors, and self-inflicted overload before the core system becomes unstable.
Application architecture and APIs Choose service boundaries, communication styles, and coordination patterns that fit both the workload and the team operating the system.
0 / 5 complete
Application layer responsibilities The application layer turns requests into business actions, coordinates data access, and owns the product semantics that infrastructure alone cannot express.Monoliths vs. microservices Choose decomposition based on coordination cost, deployment independence, and team boundaries rather than trend pressure.Service discovery and coordination Distributed services need a way to locate healthy instances, coordinate leaders, and react to topology changes without manual reconfiguration.API design: REST, RPC, and contracts Pick communication styles that match workflow complexity, evolution needs, and performance sensitivity.Real-time delivery: WebSockets, SSE, and long polling When users expect live updates, choose a delivery model that matches fan-out shape, connection count, and the direction of data flow.
Data storage and indexing Match storage engines and data models to access patterns, write volume, consistency needs, and query flexibility.
0 / 5 complete
Relational data modeling and indexing Relational databases remain the default for strongly constrained data, multi-row invariants, and flexible querying with mature operational tooling.Replication and failover Replicas improve read scale, durability, and recovery options, but they introduce lag, failover complexity, and write ownership decisions.Partitioning, federation, and sharding Break data into smaller pieces when a single machine or logical schema becomes the bottleneck for size, throughput, or ownership.The NoSQL landscape NoSQL systems trade some relational flexibility for scale, availability patterns, schema freedom, or specialized query models.Choosing the right storage mix Most serious systems use more than one storage technology. The skill is choosing a small set whose strengths align with the workload and team.
Caching, asynchrony, and resilience Reduce latency, smooth bursts, and survive failures by placing the right work in caches, queues, and resilient control loops.
0 / 5 complete
Caching layers and cache placement Caching can remove repeated work from the critical path, but only if you choose the right layer and understand freshness requirements.Cache invalidation and update patterns Pick an update pattern that matches read/write mix, freshness requirements, and operational simplicity.Queues, streams, and background work Move non-interactive or bursty work off the request path so user-facing latency stays bounded and the system can absorb spikes gracefully.Idempotency, retries, and back pressure Make distributed workflows safe under duplicate delivery, partial failure, and overload instead of assuming every call succeeds exactly once.Observability and operational feedback loops Metrics, logs, traces, and alerting turn architecture choices into something operators can actually trust and improve.
Security, multi-region, and operations Design systems that are safe to expose, recoverable during disasters, and maintainable by real teams over time.
0 / 4 complete
Security foundations for system design Security choices should appear throughout the design, not as a last-minute box labeled auth.Multi-region design and disaster recovery Use multiple regions when the business case justifies lower outage risk, lower latency, or data residency needs, while accepting the added coordination cost.Fault tolerance and graceful degradation Healthy systems keep the core user journey alive when dependencies fail by shedding optional work and falling back to simpler behavior.Deployments, capacity, and cost awareness Production systems need safe release mechanics, realistic capacity headroom, and an explicit understanding of what the architecture costs to run.
Common product design patterns Practice recurring architectures that appear across many interview prompts and real-world products.
0 / 5 complete
Feeds and timelines Feeds combine ranking, fan-out, caching, and read-time assembly decisions under heavy skewed traffic.File upload, processing, and distribution Media-heavy products require a pipeline for uploads, validation, durable storage, transformation, and fast global delivery.Search and autocomplete Search systems combine ingestion, indexing, ranking, caching, and query-time latency controls.Chat, presence, and notifications Messaging products blend low-latency delivery, persistent history, fan-out, device state, and notification fallbacks.Payments and ledger-style systems Money movement demands idempotency, auditability, reconciliation, and a conservative approach to asynchronous side effects.
Case studies and interview drills Apply the concepts to end-to-end systems that commonly appear in interviews and architecture reviews.
0 / 5 complete
Case study: URL shortener Design a service that creates short codes, resolves them quickly, tracks analytics, and avoids hot-key failures.Case study: distributed web crawler Crawlers combine frontier management, politeness, deduplication, storage, and ranking across many workers.Case study: social graph service A social graph stores user relationships, supports high-volume fan-out queries, and powers many downstream ranking and recommendation systems.Case study: query-result cache Design a cache that stores expensive query results while handling invalidation, skew, and freshness guarantees.Case study: scaling playbook Practice describing how a simple single-region web application evolves through caching, queueing, partitioning, and global distribution over multiple growth stages.
Curriculum → Application architecture and APIs → Application layer responsibilities

Lesson 1 of 5

Application layer responsibilities

The application layer turns requests into business actions, coordinates data access, and owns the product semantics that infrastructure alone cannot express.

25-35 min Application architecture and APIs

Why it matters

Interview framing

A clean application layer lets you scale capabilities independently without losing clarity around ownership and correctness.

Study section

Separate concerns intentionally

Presentation, orchestration, domain logic, and persistence concerns often blur together in early systems. Draw boundaries so the main invariants remain obvious.

  • Keep user-facing orchestration close to the business flow
  • Move reusable infrastructure concerns into libraries or shared services
  • Prefer explicit ownership over generic helper abstractions

Study section

Stateless compute scales best

Application servers should usually stay stateless and push durable state into caches, databases, or event logs.

  • Stateless services simplify balancing and failover
  • Session state should be externalized if it must survive restarts
  • Background workers often share logic with online services but have different latency goals

Study section

Coordinate around domain boundaries

Structure services and modules around business capabilities, not just technical layers, so ownership remains stable as the team grows.

  • Define clear APIs and data ownership
  • Avoid shared mutable databases across many teams
  • Use contracts and events for cross-boundary interactions

Reference diagram

Visual anchor

The application layer coordinates domain logic between clients and storage systems.

Adapted from The System Design Primer by Donne Martin (CC BY 4.0).

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Explain what the application layer owns beyond request forwarding.
  • Prefer stateless services for elasticity.
  • Align boundaries with business capabilities.
  • Keep invariants easy to locate and test.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Mixing infrastructure glue and business rules everywhere.
  • Spreading one domain invariant across many services.
  • Treating background jobs as an afterthought instead of part of the same workflow.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • What logic belongs in the application layer rather than the database or gateway?
  • How do you keep a stateless service from depending on hidden local state?
  • What changes when one domain is owned by many teams?

Next steps

Keep moving through the module

Monoliths vs. microservices →
